coredata.dc:title: A rational strategy for directly synthesizing strongly yellow solid-state fluorescent-emitting carbon nanodots composite microparticles by structure regulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiao
Li, Wenjing
Wu, Yongzhong
Li, Xiaoyan
Ma, Yan
Wang, Hangxiang
Wu, Min
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have received huge attention of researchers due to their good optical properties. However, CDs generally suffer from severely fluorescence self-quenching once they are aggregated or in solid state, limitating their applications in light-emitting devices. In this work, we report a rational strategy for synthesis of yellow fluorescent carbon nanodots (Y-CDs)-based microparticles with a high solid-state photoluminescence quantum yield (32.02%). It is the first report on designing a lamellar structured matrix to disperse CDs to realize long-wavelength solid fluorescence. The matrix is confirmed as p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride by X-ray diffraction (XRD). 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560) acts as a bridge to connect CDs to the matrix based on epoxy-amine reaction and silane hydrolysis condensation for uniform dispersion of CDs and high solid-state luminescence can be easily achieved. Meanwhile, through adjusting the amount of KH-560, the morphology of Y-CDs-based microparticles can be controlled from cube to layered structure and the fluorescence intensity can be significantly improved. Importantly, the optimal Y-CDs particles can be combined with blue chips (450 nm) to fabricate high-performance white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) without using commercial phosphor, and the constructed WLEDs exhibit a high color rendering index (CRI) of 84.2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot composite
Lamellar structure
Epoxy-amine reaction
Optoelectronic applications
Solid-state fluorescence

Preparation of the Y-CDs-based LED device
The performance stability test of LED device
Analysis of the morphology and structure of Y-CDs-based particles

Wu thanks the support by the Sichuan Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (2022JDJQ0049), and the Scientific and Technological Achievements Transformation Fund of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Grant CGZH21002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic route for the Y-CDs-based particles. The photo of Y-CDs-8 particles (b) under daylight and (c) under 365 nm UV light. The photo of the upper solution of Y-CDs-8 (d) under daylight and (e) under 365 nm UV light. (f) The UV–vis absorption, PL excitation (λem = 552 nm) and PL emission (λex = 470 nm) spectra of Y-CDs-8 particles.

TEM images of (a) Y-CDs-0, (b) Y-CDs-1, (c) Y-CDs-4, (d) Y-CDs-8, and (e) Y-CDs-10. Inset in (a) is the photo of the Y-CDs-0 particles under 365 nm light and Insets in (b–e) are corresponding size distribution. (f–i) The corresponding HRTEM images of Y-CDs-1, Y-CDs-4, Y-CDs-8, and Y-CDs-10, respectively.
SEM images of (a1, a2) Y-CDs-0, (b1, b2) Y-CDs-1, (c1, c2) Y-CDs-4, (d1, d2) Y-CDs-8, and (e1, e2) Y-CDs-10.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of the Y-CDs-based microparticles.

The Y-CDs-based particles: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) PL emission spectra (λex = 470 nm), (c) PL excitation spectra (λem = 550 nm), and (d) fluorescence decay curves. Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of (e) Y-CDs-1, (f) Y-CDs-4, (g) Y-CDs-8, and (h) Y-CDs-10.

(a) Luminescence emission spectra of yellow LEDs under 20 mA working current (inset is the photography of yellow LED). (b) CIE chromaticity coordinate of yellow LEDs. (c) Luminescence emission spectra of WLEDs under 20 mA working current (inset is the photography of WLED) (d) CIE chromaticity coordinate of the WLEDs.
Comparison of parameters relevant to silane-assisted synthesis of CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: KH-560 (3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane), AEAPMS (N -(β -aminoethyl)- γ -aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane, KH-602), APS ((3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane), AEPMDS (3-(2-aminoethylamino)propylmethyl-dimethoxysilane), LEDs (light-emitting diodes), AEAPTMS (N-(2-Aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane), KH-792 (N-(3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl) ethylenediamine), APTES (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane), AEEA (3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane), LSCs (luminescent solar concentrators).

head.ce:title: A rational strategy for directly synthesizing strongly yellow solid-state fluorescent-emitting carbon nanodots composite microparticles by structure regulation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Radiology and Huaxi MR Research Center (HMRRC)
Functional and Molecular Imaging Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have received huge attention of researchers due to their good optical properties. However, CDs generally suffer from severely fluorescence self-quenching once they are aggregated or in solid state, limitating their applications in light-emitting devices. In this work, we report a rational strategy for synthesis of yellow fluorescent carbon nanodots (Y-CDs)-based microparticles with a high solid-state photoluminescence quantum yield (32.02%). It is the first report on designing a lamellar structured matrix to disperse CDs to realize long-wavelength solid fluorescence. The matrix is confirmed as p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride by X-ray diffraction (XRD). 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560) acts as a bridge to connect CDs to the matrix based on epoxy-amine reaction and silane hydrolysis condensation for uniform dispersion of CDs and high solid-state luminescence can be easily achieved. Meanwhile, through adjusting the amount of KH-560, the morphology of Y-CDs-based microparticles can be controlled from cube to layered structure and the fluorescence intensity can be significantly improved. Importantly, the optimal Y-CDs particles can be combined with blue chips (450 nm) to fabricate high-performance white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) without using commercial phosphor, and the constructed WLEDs exhibit a high color rendering index (CRI) of 84.2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Xiao Gong: Conceptualization, review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Wenjing Li: Synthesis, Characterization, Writing – original manuscript, review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis. Yongzhou Wu: Review & editing. Xiaoyan Li: Review & editing. Yan Ma: Review & editing. Hangxiang Wang: Review & editing. Min Wu: Review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

In 2006, Sun et al. [1] firstly reported the quantum-sized fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) via laser ablation of a carbon target. Since then, CDs have received huge attention due to their specific characteristics, such as low toxicity, easy synthesis, and excellent photostability compared with organic dyes [2–4]. Although a large number of CDs exhibit excellent photoluminescence properties in aqueous solution and have been applied in bioimaging, sensing, catalysis, and solar cells [5–10], aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) hinders their further applications in solid-state. Similar to organic molecules, CDs suffer luminescence quenching when they are aggregated because of the resonance energy transfer (RET) and direct π-π interaction [11–13]. To get solid-state fluorescent (SSF) CDs, the wide strategy is to embed CDs in the matrices such as KCl [14], Ca(OH)2 [15], trisodium citrate [16], cellulose [12], metal organic framework (MOF) [3], and polymers [14–16] (e.g., polyvinyl pyrrolidone, polystyrene, silicone). However, there remain some problems in the preparation of solid-state CDs. First, the ACQ still occurs when the composites are at a high CDs loading concentration. Second, some post-processing to get SSF CDs involves rotary evaporation and freeze-drying, which is cumbersome. Third, the fluorescence emission of most high QY solid-state CDs is usually located in the blue-green region. Therefore, it is urgent to prepare high-efficiency long-wavelength emission solid-state CDs by a direct and convenient method.

To obtain SSF CDs, avoiding conjugation region or forming crosslinked polymer network structure is necessary [14,17,18]. So long-chain alkyl materials, polymers, and silane coupling agents are selected to fabricate SSF CDs [13,19–22]. Shao et al. [23] prepared self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescent polymer CDs, which avoided π-π interaction quenching due to the formed polymer net structure. Recently, silane coupling agents and citric acid have been used to construct SSF CDs through one-step methods, which are also called silane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs). Amino silane (such as 3-(aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) and N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO)) is wildly used [24,25]. Through the reaction between amino groups and carboxyl groups, the long-chain silane is modified with the CDs. At the same time, the organosilane hydrolyzes and condenses to form a network structure to disperse the CDs and realize solid-state luminescence. These methods based on the reaction between citric acid and silane coupling agent with the amino group are commonly used. However, the fluorescence emission of these SiCDs is mainly in the blue and green region [19,26–29], and SiCDs with a long wavelength emission need to be developed.

Inspired by the solid-state luminescence mechanism of these SiCDs, we chose p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) as the carbon source and 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560) to fabricate long-wavelength emission SSF CDs. Here, a facile method based on epoxy-amine reaction is proposed to one-step prepare high-efficiency yellow fluorescent CDs-based particles with a lamellar structure, which has not been reported yet. In this work, besides KH-560 and p-PD, we added concentrated hydrochloric acid to the precursor solution, which generated the solid matrix of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride. KH-560 was used as a bridge based on epoxy-amine reaction and silane hydrolysis condensation to connect CDs derived from aniline with the solid matrix of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride. What's more, the long-chain introduced by the KH-560 on p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride affected the morphology of the matrics, which caused the lamellar structure. Therefore, by adjusting the ratio of p-PD and KH-560, the morphology and the fluorescence quantum yield of the particles could be controlled. Finally, we obtained the optimal Y-CDs-8 particles with a solid-state fluorescence quantum yield of 32.02%. The Y-CDs-8 particles were adhered to blue chips to construct WLEDs. The Commission Internationale de I'Elcairage coordinate (CIE) was (0.364, 0.312) and the color rendering index was 84.2.

P-phenylenediamine (p-PD, 98%) and 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560, 97%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Ethanol (99.7%) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36%–38%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The 365 nm chips (1 W) and 450 nm chips (5 W) were purchased from Guhoon Optoelectronics Co., Ltd. The UV structural adhesives were purchased from Shenzhen Tegu New materials Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of yellow fluorescent CDs (Y-CDs)-based microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: 0.108 g p-phenylenediamine dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and an appropriate amount of KH-560 and 1 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid was added to the solution. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined reaction kettle, which was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h and cooled naturally to room temperature. Next, the solution was centrifuged with 10000 rpm for 5 min and the precipitate was washed with ethanol until the supernatant had no fluorescence. Finally, the particles were obtained after drying. According to the molar ratio of p-phenylenediamine to KH-560 of 1:0, 1:1, 1:4, 1:8, and 1:10, the yellow fluorescent CDs-based microparticles are recorded as Y-CDs-0, Y-CDs-1, Y-CDs-4, Y-CDs-8, and Y-CDs-10 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Y-CDs-based LED device
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, Y-CDs-based microparticles were mixed with UV curable adhesive for packaging (the mass ratio of the powder and adhesive: 1:1). The 365 nm chip (1 W) and 450 nm chip (5 W) were selected for packaging to construct monochrome LEDs and WLEDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance stability test of LED device
ce:section.ce:para: The working current stability test: under 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 160, 200, 240, 280, and 320 mA, the performance of the prepared devices were tested.
The working time stability test: the prepared devices were powered on for a while under 20 mA, and the performance was recorded.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) characterization was carried out by JEM-2100F TEM (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) diagrams were performed using Zeiss Ultra Plus. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by Nicolet 6700. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a D8 DISCOVER (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out using AXIS SUPRA. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained by Lambda 750 S (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed using QM/TM/NIR (PTI, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes and absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were measured by EI FLS1000 Photoluminescence Spectrometer. The electroluminescence (EL) spectra of LEDs were measured using OHSP-350 M LED Fast-Scan Spectrophotometer (Hangzhou Hopoo Light & Color Technology Co., Ltd).

The Y-CDs-based microparticles were prepared by a one-step solvothermal approach using p-PD, KH-560, and HCl (36%–38%) in ethanol (Fig. 1 a). In most work, SSF CDs were obtained via subsequent hydrolysis-condensation and drying after the hydrothermal or solvothermal methods. In this work, our approach is much easier and the Y-CDs-based microparticles are insoluble and can be obtained directly by centrifugation (Fig. 1b and c). The Y-CDs-8 microparticles show the maximum emission at 552 nm under excitation at 470 nm. The absorption spectra indicate the bands at ∼ 262, 305, and 435 nm (Fig. 1f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the morphology and structure of Y-CDs-based particles
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs are not found in the TEM image of the Y-CDs-0 microparticles prepared without KH-560. Fig. S1 shows there are many fragment particles because the powder was dispersed in ethanol and the supernatant was taken for testing after ultrasonic for a while. While CDs can be found in Fig. 2b–e under the same sample preparation method and test condition. Inset in Fig. 2a shows the physical picture of the Y-CDs-0 particles under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp and the microparticles have no fluorescence. From the TEM diagrams of the other four Y-CDs, it is observed that the CDs are uniformly dispersed and relatively homogeneous in size, showing a sphere-like morphology. Fig. 2f–i shows the corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of the CDs, and the crystallographic spacing of all four CDs is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite. Insets in Fig. 2b–e are the corresponding particle size distribution diagrams of Y-CDs-1, Y-CDs-4, Y-CDs-8, and Y-CDs-10. Y-CDs-1 are distributed between 1.5 and 5.2 nm, with an average size of 2.43 ± 0.06 nm; Y-CDs-4 are distributed between 1.2 and 4.4 nm, with an average size of 2.74 ± 0.10 nm; Y-CDs-8 are distributed between 2.4 and 5.0 nm, with an average size of 3.03 ± 0.09 nm; Y-CDs-10 are distributed between 2.5 and 5.0 nm, and the average size is 3.64 ± 0.06 nm. With the increase of KH-560 amount, the average size of CDs becomes larger, which might be due to the more silane coupling agent participation in the compounding of CDs with the matrix material. In addition, the upper solution after centrifugation of all the samples shows yellow fluorescence under 365 nm light as the upper solution of the Y-CDs-8 sample (Fig. 1e), and the solid particle powder also shows yellow fluorescence. Even for the sample without KH-560, the upper solution also has yellow fluorescence. Combined with this experimental phenomenon and the TEM results, we speculate that the solid-state fluorescence originates from CDs derived from p-PD and the addition of KH-560 contributes to the generation of solid-state fluorescence.

To further investigate the structural composition of the particles, the results of SEM characterization analysis of the particles are shown in Fig. 3 . It should be noted that these microparticles are not very uniform, which may be due to the preparation conditions of high temperature and high pressure. In Fig. 3a, the Y-CDs-0 particle is a short stick shape with a smooth surface and a size of about 10 μm. When KH-560 is added, the morphology of the bulk solid changes significantly from cubic to lamellar structure, and the surface shows an obvious peeled shape. When p-PD: KH-560 = 1:1, the particle shape is the irregular block with a size of ∼ 50 μm, and the surface has slight strips of peeled traces. When the ratio changed to 1:4, the particle size is about 40 μm and the particles appear more obvious lamellar structure. In Fig. 3d, the particle morphology presents a tendency for lamellar shape, and a large number of square depressions appear on the surface. However, when the amount of KH-560 continues to increase, the large amount of long-chain structure of KH-560 causes more obstruction to the original growth of the microparticles, making it hard to produce large-sized particles and many small fragments appear. Therefore, a large number of fragments within 10 μm are shown in Fig. 3e and the presence of many lamellar fragments and rectangular depressions on its surface can be noticed. It is assumed that KH-560 encapsulated or attached CDs to large-size bulk particles, which is the origin of solid-state fluorescence. The long-chain structure of KH-560 would affect the particle growth and morphology of the solid matrix to a certain extent.

The FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 4 a, from which it is obvious that Y-CDs-1, Y-CDs-4, Y-CDs-8, and Y-CDs-10 have significant Si–H stretching vibrations (around 2225 cm−1), Si–O stretching vibrations at 1062 cm−1 and Si–C stretching vibrations around 827 cm−1 due to the addition of KH-560. The strong N–H stretching vibration peak around 3370 cm−1 of Y-CDs-0 indicates that the amino groups occupied more compared with the other four samples. In contrast to Y-CDs-0, the FTIR spectra of Y-CDs-1, Y-CDs-4, Y-CDs-8, and Y-CDs-10 show a stronger vibration peak around 1311 cm−1, which is attributed to the C–N stretching vibration of secondary amine, suggesting that the epoxy groups of KH-560 reacted with the amino groups of p-PD to form secondary amine. KH-560 could react with the amino of CDs and the long-chain silane grafted on CDs, which is helpful for KH-560 to disperse CDs and avoid fluorescence quenching due to close distance. The XRD spectra of the five samples particles are similar (Fig. 4b) and match the PDF card of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride (PDF: 39–1793), which indicates that the crystalline particles in the five samples are all p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride. After p-PD is completely dissolved in ethanol, p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride is generated by adding HCl. The KH-560 added afterward reacts with p-PD, part of them reacts with p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride, and the other part reacts with p-PD in solution. Under high temperature and pressure, CDs with long-chain silane are generated, and the organosilane part is hydrolyzed and condensed, acting as a "bridge" to connect CDs with p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride particles and resulting in solid luminescence from CDs dispersed in the matrix. As shown in Fig. S2a, except Y-CDs-0, the other four particles contain Si elements, which is due to the addition of KH-560. These five particles contain C, N, O, Cl elements. In Fig. S3, the contents of C–Si and C–O are gradually increasing with the increase of KH-560, indicating that more KH-560 was involved in the generation of Y-CDs-based microparticles, which is consistent with the inferred results of TEM and SEM analysis. By comparing the N 1s fine spectra of the five particles (Fig. S4), it is found that the addition of KH-560 promoted the increase of pyridinic N and amine N contents, speculatively because the KH-560 participation reduced the degree of self polymerization of the p-PD monomer, and some p-PD participated in the reaction with KH-560. Due to the addition of KH-560, a large amount of O element is introduced into the particles. It is clearly shown in the O 1s fine spectra of the five particles (Fig. S5) that an increase in O–Si/C–OH, again proving the involvement of KH-560 in the generation of particles.

Analyzing the above test results, it was the addition of KH-560 that affected the morphology of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride growth. Because the epoxy group of KH-560 reacted with the amino group of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride, the long-chain structure was grafted on the particles, which would hinder the growth of the crystal along a certain direction and cause the formation and increase of the lamellar structure. What's more, when more KH-560 participated in the reaction, more long-chain structures were introduced, which would lead to the formation of fragments rather than blocky-shaped particles.

To investigate the effect of KH-560 addition on the optical properties of the particles, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the five particles were tested. It is found that except for the absorption of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride, an absorption peak appears at around 450 nm of the fluorescent microparticles. This absorption peak corresponds to the visible absorption of CDs, which is assigned to the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN [30] (Fig. 5 a). Under the excitation at 470 nm, Y-CDs-0 particles show no fluorescence, and the other four particles all have fluorescence peaks at 552 nm with a broad full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ∼ 107 nm (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5e–h are the fluorescence excitation-dependent spectra of the remaining four particles, respectively. The excitement-dependent phenomenon of these four CDs is not obvious. It could deduce by the excitement-independent emission that the four CDs have homogeneous surface states and narrow size distribution. The similar optical properties reveal that series Y-CDs-based microparticles have the same fluorescence emission center. The fluorescence emission mechanism is more likely to be based on surface states rather than the quantum size effect for the fact that the four different size CDs exhibit similar fluorescent properties. The results of the fitted fluorescence lifetimes curves are presented in Fig. 5d, and the fluorescence lifetimes of Y-CDs-1, Y-CDs-4, Y-CDs-8, and Y-CDs-10 particles are 4.28 ns, 4.90 ns, 6.42 ns, and 5.37 ns, respectively. Among the four particles, Y-CDs-8 has the longest average lifetime and the highest PLQY of 32.02% (Table S2). The PLQYs and the corresponding emission wavelengths were significantly enhanced compared to the previously reported silane-assisted synthesis of CDs, as shown in Table 1 . In order to study the optimum parameters for the experiment, different reaction temperatures and reaction times were investigated. Figs. S6a and S6b show the optical pictures of CDs under different reaction times, and the corresponding solid-state yields of CDs increased with the increase of reaction time and the fluorescence showed a significant enhancement. When the reaction time was 4 h, the reaction time reached the optimum, and by continuing to increase the reaction time, the yield of CDs began to decrease, and the fluorescence intensity also decreased. Similarly, different reaction temperatures had similar results with reaction time. The optical pictures of CDs at different reaction temperatures are demonstrated as shown in Figs. S6c and S6d. An increase in temperature leads to an increase in fluorescence intensity, but when the temperature is higher than 180 °C, the reaction starts without solid production. Besides, the effect of Y-CDs in different polar solvents was also investigated. CDs have different solubility in different solvents and are not soluble in most of the solvents. Moreover, the fluorescence of CDs in different solvents hardly produced any change (Fig. S7). From the 2 ns components in Table S1 and the above analysis, we deduce that the addition of KH-560 would not affect the fluorescence emission center and it plays an important role in adjusting the dispersion of CDs in the matrix. As KH-560 increases, more organosilanes react with p-PD, hydrolyzed and condensed to wrap more CDs. However, KH-560 can not be added indefinitely. Y-CDs-10 shows a decrease in PLQY and lifetime. Because excessive KH-560 leads to a large number of fragments in the solid particles and excessive loading of CDs. This makes the distance between CDs closer thus increasing nonradiative recombination and energy loss [31,32], which may cause RET and shorten the lifetime [33–35].

The optimal Y-CDs-8 particles were used with different chips to build monochromatic and WLEDs. Y-CDs-8 has wide FWHM (∼ 107 nm), which covers the more red region and is conductive to fabricate WLEDs with blue chips [42,43]. From Fig. 6 a, it is found that the emitted light is mainly in the long-wavelength region and the emission peak is located around 603 nm. The change of peak position may be related to the influence of polymer during the packaging process [44–46]. The CIE coordinate of the monochromatic yellow LEDs is located at (0.503, 0.475), with a color temperature of 2619 K and a color rendering index of 70.1. With the enhancement of the current, its peak intensity gradually increases. When increasing to 320 mA, the peak intensity has a slight decline, and the peak position does not change during the process. This phenomenon indicates that the device has good current stability (Fig. S8a). The change of luminous flux intensity within 2 h working time on the device was recorded (Fig. S8b). On the other hand, the Y-CDS-8 particles can be used with blue chips to prepare WLEDs. In the luminescence spectrum (Fig. 6c), in addition to the 450 nm emission peak of chips, there is an emission peak at about 600 nm. The two emission peaks together make up the white light emission spectrum. The CIE coordinate of the device is (0.364, 0.312), the color temperature is 3935 K for white light, and the color rendering index is 84.2, which indicates the potential of these CDs-based microparticles for WLEDs applications. Likewise, the device was tested for current stability and time stability. With the increase of working currents, the peak intensity gradually increases, indicating good current stability (Fig. S8c). The intensity of the luminous flux decreases slightly with the increase of the working time and reaches about 75% of the initial one after 2 h (Fig. S8d).

In summary, the high-performance Y-CDs-based microparticles were directly obtained via a one-step solvothermal method. Based on TEM, SEM, XRD, and FTIR analyses, the solid-state fluorescent microparticles were composed of p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride matrix and CDs. To find out the effect of KH-560 on Y-CDs-based microparticles formation, the morphology and optical properties of these particles were investigated by changing the ratio of KH-560 and p-PD. The morphology of Y-CDs-based microparticles changed from cubic to lamellar structure and the PLQY gradually increased from 11.2% to 32.02%. The more KH-560 would wrap more CDs to the matrix. The long-chain silane on the matrix hindered the growth of the crystal in a certain direction and caused the formation and increase of the lamellar structure. But excessive KH-560 would lead to wrapping too many CDs, making the distance between CDs closer and nonradiative recombination increase and generating many fragments. The optimal Y-CDs-8 microparticles were obtained with the lamellar structure matrix and the high solid-state PLQY of 32.02%. These solid-state fluorescent microparticles show the optimum emission peak is located at 552 nm excited by 470 nm, the FWHW of which is ∼ 107 nm. Therefore, we successfully constructed the WLEDs without commercial phosphor by combining Y-CDs-8 microparticles with blue chips (450 nm) due to these fluorescence properties. The CIE coordinate of WLEDs was (0.364, 0.312), the color temperature was 3935 K, and the color rendering index was 84.2. In addition, the device showed great current and working time stability. The CD-based phosphor provides a more green and simpler choice for the construction of photoluminescence LEDs. This work proposed a new strategy to prepare the high-efficiency long-wavelength emission solid-state fluorescent CDs-based particles with lamellar structure and revealed the promising potential in WLEDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098). M. Wu thanks the support by the Sichuan Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (2022JDJQ0049), and the Scientific and Technological Achievements Transformation Fund of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Grant CGZH21002).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtadv.2023.100428.
